title: Mission
text: The Cognavitron — hover for details · click to navigate

title: Mission
text:
The Vision
Imagine a world where local communities have access to the tools, data, and scientific support to understand and protect the ecosystems in which they live.
Networks of community-based ecological monitoring systems where citizen scientists aren’t just data suppliers — they define the questions, gather data, advocate for protection, and drive conservation and management.
A picture of your local ecosystem, situated within the broader one. Local communities bring what no remote sensor captures: lived knowledge of the place, generational observation, the kind of noticing that comes from being there. The science provides the analytical infrastructure and the broader context. Each informs the other.

title: Mission
section: What This Looks Like in Practice
text:
What This Looks Like in Practice
To Be Completed.

title: Mission
text:
Training as Mission Infrastructure
The challenge before us cannot be met by a handful of people who know these tools. We need ecologists, conservation scientists, tribal environmental staff, and STEM students who understand not just AI, but the ecology- and conservation-specific aspects of applying it.

title: Mission
text:
Listening to the Earth
At the core of this vision is service of something bigger: a genuine relationship between people and the living systems they (and we) depend on.
The sensors and the models are expressions of care, not replacements for it. We can be technically sophisticated and still be creatures of this planet — embedded in it, accountable to it.
If any of this resonates with you — as a researcher, funder, student, collaborator, or someone who simply cares about wild places — get in touch.

title: About
text:
My Story
Like many ecologists, I was fascinated with the natural world as far back as I can rememeber. I spent a lot of my free time exploring the forests, lakes, streams, and wetlands around our home in Northern Michigan.






Horn shark (Heterodontus francisci), San Diego kelp forest. (Photo: Jeff A. Tracey)

title: BirdNET Wrapper — Bioacoustic Inference Pipeline
text:
The Cognavitron — hover for details · click to navigate




BirdNET is a deep learning model for bird call detection and species identification in passive acoustic recordings, developed by the Cornell Lab of Ornithology and Chemnitz University of Technology. It’s excellent — but running it on a directory full of AudioMoth or Song Meter files, and getting the results into a usable table, takes a bit of scaffolding.
I built easy_birdnet for my own PAM workflows and decided to share it. It wraps the BirdNET Python package to handle batch processing, output formatting, and per-file detection plots with minimal setup. Give it a folder of audio files; get back a CSV and a plot for each one.

GitHub: https://github.com/Jeff-Tracey/easy_birdnet
License: RAIL Non-Commercial

title: AI and HPC for Ecology
text:
The Cognavitron — hover for details · click to navigate
As a computational scientist at USGS Advanced Research Computing (ARC), I spent a decade building HPC and AI/ML infrastructure for ecological research — writing parallel code, porting workflows to supercomputers, training scientists to use them, and eventually bringing deep learning into the mix. ARC operated some of the most capable scientific computing resources in the federal government, purpose-built for the kinds of large-scale ecological and environmental problems that desktop machines can’t touch.

title: AI and HPC for Ecology
text:
The Machines
ARC’s first supercomputer was Yeti — an IBM system that set the foundation for what became a serious federal HPC program for ecological science. It has since been decommissioned.



Yeti — USGS ARC’s first supercomputer. An IBM system that established the foundation for federal HPC in ecological research. Now decommissioned. (Photo: Jeff A. Tracey)


Yeti was followed by Denali and Tallgrass — two Cray systems that brought substantially more compute power and enabled the kinds of large-scale AI/ML workflows that are now central to ecological research.



Denali (center, Denali mountain graphic) and Tallgrass (left, zebra-stripe panels) — USGS ARC Cray supercomputers. The USGS logo is visible on the Tallgrass cabinet at left. These systems supported large-scale ecological simulation, movement modeling, and deep learning workflows. (Photo: Jeff Falgout, USGS)

title: Monitoring Design and Statistical Power
text:
The Cognavitron — hover for details · click to navigate
A recurring thread across projects: how do you design a monitoring program that can actually detect what you need to detect, in time to act on it? Power analysis and study design show up across species and contexts — wood frogs in Colorado, reptiles and amphibians in San Diego, long-term USGS monitoring programs. The answer is usually the same: it takes longer than you think, costs more than you planned, and the statistical design matters as much as the field effort.
More content coming soon.

title: Monitoring Design and Statistical Power
text:
Projects
Wood frog egg mass surveys — Simulation study to determine how many years of monitoring are required to detect a specified decline in wood frog (Lithobates sylvaticus) populations using counts of egg masses. Stochastic population model combined with a survey simulation framework. With Rick Scherer (Colorado State University).

title: Golden Eagles — Telemetry and Occupancy
text:
The Cognavitron — hover for details · click to navigate
GPS telemetry data for golden eagles (Aquila chrysaetos) in southern California, visualized in three dimensions using ParaView. The study area spans the urban-wildland interface east of San Diego — chaparral, oak woodland, and grassland habitat embedded in a matrix of urban and agricultural land use.
The video below shows the release of eagle M001 on Otay Mountain after fitting with a GPS telemetry unit. With Pete Bloom. (Video: Jeff A. Tracey)
Releasing eagle M001 on Otay Mountain, San Diego County. 5 December 2014.

title: Golden Eagles — Telemetry and Occupancy
text:
Field Methods
Eagles were captured using a dho-gaza trap with a live great horned owl lure — a technique that exploits the golden eagle’s territorial response to its primary nest competitor. Pete Bloom, one of the foremost raptor biologists in the western US, led all trapping. Once captured, eagles were fitted with a solar-powered GPS telemetry unit mounted on a backpack harness, weighed, measured, and released at the capture site.



Pete Bloom holding a freshly captured golden eagle prior to fitting the telemetry unit. Pete led all eagle trapping for the study. Otay Mountain, San Diego County. (Photo: Jeff A. Tracey 2014)





Jeff Tracey holding a golden eagle on Otay Mountain. The red hood keeps the bird calm during handling. Urban San Diego is visible in the background — the urban-wildland interface that defined the study area. (Photo: Pete Bloom)





Pete Bloom and Jeff Tracey fitting a GPS telemetry unit to a golden eagle on Otay Mountain. The solar panel on the harness keeps the unit charged; data are transmitted via satellite. (Photo: field crew)





The solar-powered GPS telemetry unit fitted to a golden eagle. The unit logs high-resolution GPS locations and transmits them via satellite — no need to retrieve the bird to download data. (Photo: Jeff A. Tracey 2014)





Golden eagle talons — Otay Mountain, San Diego County. These feet can exert several hundred pounds per square inch of force; the primary tools of a sit-and-wait predator that takes prey as large as deer fawns. (Photo: Jeff A. Tracey 2014)

title: Golden Eagles — Telemetry and Occupancy
text:
The Study
This project grew out of a need to understand how golden eagles were using the urban-wildland landscape east of San Diego — a mosaic of chaparral, grassland, and oak woodland pressed against one of the fastest-growing urban edges in California.
I got the study off the ground in 2014 with Jeremy Sebes, a field technician, with critical help from Pete Bloom — one of the foremost raptor biologists in the western US — who led all the eagle trapping. Pete’s expertise was essential; capturing a golden eagle is not something you improvise. The first bird we fitted with a GPS unit was M001, a female we caught on Otay Mountain in December 2014.
For a few years I supervised the field work while also managing the data pipeline and doing the analysis. Eventually Melanie Madden — an actual bird biologist — took over field supervision, which was exactly right. I shifted fully to data management, modeling, and the computational side of the project. That division of labor is how these things should work: field expertise and analytical expertise in the same room, clearly distinguished.
The study area centered on Otay Mountain and the surrounding wildlands — Rancho Jamul Ecological Reserve, San Diego National Wildlife Refuge — with urban Chula Vista and Otay Ranch pressing in from the west.



Otay Mountain from the lower slopes, looking north toward the summit. The communications tower on the ridge is a landmark visible from much of the study area. This is chaparral and coastal sage scrub habitat — dry, open, and rocky, the kind of terrain golden eagles hunt over. (Photo: Jeff A. Tracey 2014)





M001’s home range over Otay Mountain and surrounding wildlands, shown on 2014 Google Earth imagery. Urban Chula Vista and Otay Ranch are visible at left — the development pressure that motivated the habitat selection analysis. Rancho Jamul Ecological Reserve and San Diego National Wildlife Refuge occupy the wildland block in the center. (Image: Google Earth 2014)

title: Golden Eagles — Telemetry and Occupancy
text:
Study Area
The terrain and land cover context for the eagle telemetry study, visualized in ParaView from a digital elevation model with land use/land cover overlay. Urban areas in gray; natural vegetation in green; developed/disturbed areas in orange.



Top-down view of the study area terrain and land use/land cover. Urban San Diego is visible at left; wildland habitat extends to the east.





Oblique view of the same landscape, showing terrain relief. The urban-wildland interface is clearly visible — eagles move between natural and human-modified habitats across this gradient.

title: Golden Eagles — Telemetry and Occupancy
text:
3D Telemetry Visualization
GPS location data for eagle M001, visualized in three spatial dimensions over the study area terrain. These visualizations were produced using ParaView with custom R code to export GPS data and MKDE outputs in VTK format.



All GPS locations for eagle M001, rendered as points over the 3D terrain surface. The dense cluster of locations reflects core use areas within the home range.





All movement steps for eagle M001 rendered as white tubes in 3D space over the terrain. The vertical extent of the tubes captures the eagle’s use of altitude.





GPS locations where eagle M001 was on or near the ground, represented as spheres scaled by and colored by first-passage time. Larger, warmer-colored spheres indicate locations where the eagle spent more time in area-restricted search — likely foraging patches.





Top-down view of low ground-speed GPS locations for eagle M001. Low ground speed indicates perching or foraging behavior; the spatial clustering of these locations reveals habitat features associated with resting and foraging activity.





GPS tracks for eagle M001 colored by ground speed (m/s). Faster movements in warmer colors; slower, area-restricted movements in cooler colors. The pattern reveals distinct commuting corridors and foraging areas.

title: Golden Eagles — Telemetry and Occupancy
text:
Animation
Movement of eagle M001 animated over time, rendered in 3D over the study area terrain.


Your browser does not support the video tag.

title: Golden Eagles — Telemetry and Occupancy
text:
Habitat Selection and Urban Avoidance
Golden eagles in San Diego County strongly avoid urban areas, with habitat use increasing sharply with distance from development. The relationship is non-linear: use probability rises steeply from the urban edge, peaks around 2km out, then levels off into wildland habitat.



Probability of use as a function of distance to urban land cover. Eagles strongly avoid the immediate urban edge, with peak use probability at ~2km from development. The gray band shows uncertainty across bootstrap replicates. (Tracey et al. 2018)





Spatial distribution of predicted urban avoidance across San Diego County. Teal areas indicate low-avoidance wildland habitat; warm colors indicate high-avoidance zones near urban development. The fragmented nature of remaining eagle habitat is clearly visible. (Tracey et al. 2018)


Urban development in the study area grew substantially over the study period and is projected to continue expanding. The figure below shows how urban and exurban landcover changed from 1990 to 2014 and is projected to change by 2050, alongside the predicted log-probability of eagle habitat selection — the suitable habitat base is shrinking.



Urban and exurban development (left column) and log-probability of eagle habitat selection (right column) in 1990, 2014, and projected 2050. As development expands, the spatial extent of high-quality eagle habitat contracts. (Tracey et al. 2018)

title: Golden Eagles — Telemetry and Occupancy
text:
What Happened Next
The habitat selection analysis documented something that was already visible on the ground: the wildland east of San Diego was under serious development pressure, and golden eagles were responding to it. Nearly 70% of the Proctor Valley area — part of M001’s home range, sitting between San Diego National Wildlife Refuge and Rancho Jamul Ecological Reserve — faced development that would have fragmented the remaining coastal sage scrub and severed wildlife connectivity across the landscape.
In January 2024, The Nature Conservancy, California Department of Fish and Wildlife, California Wildlife Conservation Board, and U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service jointly acquired 1,291 acres in Proctor Valley, protecting that link and connecting over 60,000 acres of contiguous wildland. Golden eagles are among the species that use the area for foraging habitat.
The acquisition was led in part by Scott Morrison, director of conservation science for TNC in California — someone I knew, though I didn’t know where he had landed. It’s good to know where he landed.
Press release: USFWS, January 2024

title: Training
text:
The Cognavitron — hover for details · click to navigate
Teaching is part of the mission. The tools and methods I use for ecological research are not yet accessible to most of the people who need them — field ecologists, conservation practitioners, tribal environmental staff, agency scientists. Closing that gap is not a side project. It’s infrastructure for the larger vision.
Every course here was designed for people with ecological knowledge but not a machine learning background. The goal is to produce practitioners who can apply these tools to real problems — not just run scripts they don’t understand.
To request a workshop or talk for your organization, get in touch.

title: Training
text:
Talks

AI 101: What Ecologists Need to Know About Artificial Intelligence
A non-technical introduction to AI for ecologists, conservation scientists, and the people who fund and manage their work. No equations, no code — just a clear-eyed look at what these tools actually do, where they genuinely help, and where they fail. Originally developed for senior leadership audiences; now offered freely to ecology and conservation organizations.
Audience: Managers, program officers, funders, field scientists curious about AI Format: 60–90 minute talk + Q&A Prerequisites: None
In development (converting from PowerPoint) — available on request for non-profit, conservation, and academic audiences.

title: Training
text:
Workshops

The Honest Ecological ML Workflow
A full-pipeline workshop covering everything most ML courses skip: study design, field data collection, annotation, dataset construction, modeling, evaluation, deployment, and communication. Designed for wildlife practitioners who have ecological data and want to use AI effectively — not just run someone else’s model.
Audience: Wildlife biologists, conservation practitioners, agency staff, NGO researchers Format: 2–3 day intensive (in-person) or split across multiple half-days (remote) Prerequisites: Basic Python familiarity helpful but not required
In development — available for institutional booking.



Scientific Python for Ecologists
Python for field scientists: environment setup, data wrangling with pandas, visualization, and batch processing of ecological data files. Every example uses real ecological data — no generic datasets. A prerequisite companion for the deep learning workshops.
Audience: Field biologists and ecologists new to Python Format: Half-day or full-day workshop Prerequisites: None
Available on request.



Scientific R for Ecologists
R for ecological data analysis: data wrangling, visualization, and statistical modeling with ecologically relevant examples. Developed and workshop-tested at USGS.
Audience: Field biologists and ecologists new to R, or R users wanting to modernize their workflow Format: Half-day or full-day workshop Prerequisites: None
Available on request.



Introduction to Deep Learning with PyTorch
Hands-on introduction to deep learning using PyTorch, taught entirely with ecological examples. Neural network fundamentals, training loops, transfer learning, and evaluation — from first principles to a working classifier on your own data.
Audience: Ecologists and conservation scientists with basic Python skills Format: 1–2 day workshop Prerequisites: Basic Python (Scientific Python workshop or equivalent)
In development.



Introduction to Image Classification with PyTorch
CNNs and transfer learning applied to ecological image data — camera trap images, drone imagery, and spectrograms. Fine-tuning pretrained models, handling class imbalance, and evaluating classifiers on held-out field data.
Audience: Ecologists working with camera traps, drones, or acoustic monitoring Format: 1–2 day workshop Prerequisites: Introduction to Deep Learning, or equivalent
In development.



Deep Learning for Bioacoustics
End-to-end acoustic monitoring pipeline: PAM data collection, spectrogram generation, BirdNET and custom classifier workflows, evaluation, and deployment. Built around real passive acoustic monitoring datasets.
Audience: Ecologists and conservation scientists working with acoustic data Format: 1–2 day workshop Prerequisites: Introduction to Deep Learning, or equivalent
In development.

To discuss bringing a workshop or talk to your organization, contact me.

title: Repos
text:
R Packages

mkde — Movement Kernel Density Estimation
R package for 2D and 3D movement-based kernel density estimation (MKDE) for animal home range and space use analysis. Developed in conjunction with the California condor 3D MKDE project; now maintained by the San Diego Supercomputer Center (SDSC).

CRAN: https://cran.r-project.org/package=mkde
Paper: Tracey JA, Sheppard J, Zhu J, Wei F, Swaisgood RR, Fisher RN (2014). Movement-Based Estimation and Visualization of Space Use in 3D for Wildlife Ecology and Conservation. PLOS ONE 9(7): e101205. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0101205
Project: California Condor Space Use
Tutorial: Animal Space Use in 2D and 3D (coming soon)

title: Repos
text:
Python Tools

easy_birdnet
Convenience wrapper for BirdNET bird call detection. Runs BirdNET on one or more audio files from AudioMoth or Song Meter recorders and produces a CSV table and detection plot per file. Supports batch and recursive directory modes.

GitHub: https://github.com/Jeff-Tracey/easy_birdnet
License: RAIL Non-Commercial



uav_pytools
Python utilities for DJI UAV mission planning, data management, and project setup. Developed for ecological field work with the DJI Mavic 3M (multispectral) and DJI Air 3S. Includes tools for GSD calculation, flight path export, image QC, RGB/multispectral splitting for WebODM, SRT telemetry parsing, and project directory management.

GitHub: https://github.com/Jeff-Tracey/uav_pytools
License: RAIL Non-Commercial



MegaDetector Pipeline
Coming soon.

title: Repos
text:
R Examples

r_large_raster
R examples for working with large raster datasets that don’t fit in memory — tiling, Virtual Raster Datasets (VRTs), and parallel processing. Developed for NAIP multispectral imagery using terra and stars. Includes two parallel implementations (base parallel and future/future.apply) and HPC examples using MPI.

GitHub: https://github.com/Jeff-Tracey/r_large_raster
License: RAIL Non-Commercial

title: California Condors — 3D Space Use
text:
2018-01-01
The Cognavitron — hover for details · click to navigate

title: California Condors — 3D Space Use
text:
Background
Biotelemetry — placing tracking devices on animals to collect location data — lets ecologists understand which areas individual animals use. GPS tracking devices can collect hundreds or thousands of locations on a schedule chosen by researchers. But even with dense GPS data, we don’t know where an animal was between observations. This uncertainty matters: an animal may have used an area we never observed it in simply because we weren’t sampling at that moment.
Ecologists have developed progressively better methods for estimating animal space use from location data. An early approach, still used today, is the minimum convex polygon (MCP) — equivalent to stretching a rubber band around all observed locations. The MCP is simple but includes many areas the animal never used. For nearly two decades after Worton (1989), kernel density estimation (KDE) based on observed locations was the standard approach. In 2007, two important advances followed: the LoCoH (local convex hull) method (Getz et al. 2007) greatly improved home range delineation over the MCP, and Horne et al. (2007) introduced a paradigm shift — estimating space use from an interpolated movement path between observed locations using a Brownian bridge, rather than from the locations themselves. This approach is called a movement-based kernel density estimate (MKDE), and it better captures areas an animal likely used during unobserved intervals.



Figure 1: Four common methods for describing animal space use. Red points are observed locations. Nonparametric methods (top row): minimum convex polygon and LoCoH, with different convex hulls in shades of gray. Kernel-based methods (bottom row): higher density regions in darker gray.


All of these methods share a common limitation: they operate in two spatial dimensions. Some species, however, have an important vertical component to their space use. Until this project, very little work had extended 2D methods to handle full 3D location data.

title: California Condors — 3D Space Use
text:
Estimating Animal Space Use in 3D
This work began as a collaboration between Jeff A. Tracey and Dr. James K. Sheppard of the San Diego Zoo’s Institute for Conservation Research (now San Diego Zoo Wildlife Alliance) to extend the MKDE approach to three spatial dimensions. The motivation was the spatial ecology of reintroduced California condors (Gymnogyps californianus) and risks they face from wind energy development along the US-Mexico border. The methods, however, apply to any species that can be tracked with biotelemetry and has significant vertical movement — birds, marine mammals, sea turtles, arboreal species.



Figure 2: A California condor in flight. (photo Don Graham, CC BY-SA 2.0)


The proposed Sempra wind farm in Baja, Mexico, sits in a corridor used by condors reintroduced by the San Diego Zoo. One bird released in Baja flew through the proposed installation area in 2007.



Figure 3: 3D model of a proposed wind farm along the US-Mexico border in Baja, Mexico. Turbine locations, tower heights, and rotor radii are consistent with the proposed installation.

title: California Condors — 3D Space Use
text:
Methodology
We extend the Brownian bridge MKDE of Horne et al. (2007) to three spatial dimensions. The animal’s movement path is approximated by linear interpolation between observed locations. A trivariate kernel is integrated over time for each move step between consecutive locations. As time since the nearest observation increases — reaching its maximum at the midpoint between consecutive locations — the variance of the kernel increases, reflecting greater positional uncertainty. The model assumes equal variance in the x- and y-dimensions and a separate variance in the z-dimension, with additional parameters for observation error. Lower and upper barriers to movement in z can also be specified — for condors, the lower barrier is the earth’s surface.



Figure 4: Steps involved in building and visualizing a 3D MKDE.


Once the 3D MKDE is calculated, contour surfaces at specified probability levels (e.g., 50%, 75%, 95%, 99%) describe the volume of space used with corresponding probability. These can be visualized in 3D using tools such as ParaView, VisIt, or Mayavi.



Figure 5: Condor movement data (red spheres = observed locations, red lines = interpolated path) with 99%, 95%, 75%, and 50% contours of a 3D MKDE.





Figure 6: Side view of condor movement data and 3D MKDE contours.

title: California Condors — 3D Space Use
text:
High-Performance Computing
The computations involved in 3D MKDE are demanding. In 2013, this collaboration expanded to include Robert Sinkovits, Amit Chourasia, and Glenn Lockwood at the San Diego Supercomputer Center (SDSC). SDSC provided technical expertise including optimization of the core C++ code, parallelization of the algorithms, access to HPC resources, and scientific visualization support.



Gordon supercomputer at SDSC. (Photo: Alan Decker / SDSC)


A paper describing the computational aspects of the methodology was presented at the XSEDE14 conference, along with an animation for the conference’s Visualization Showcase. In this visualization, 3D MKDEs for a breeding pair of California condors were calculated within a two-day moving window over several years on SDSC’s Gordon supercomputer and animated using VisIt software.

title: California Condors — 3D Space Use
text:
Software
This work produced the mkde R package, developed in collaboration with SDZG and SDSC. The package provides functions for setting up animal location data, estimating MKDE variance parameters, creating MKDE objects, computing the MKDE, and exporting output files. Joint space-use between two animals based on spatiotemporal overlap of their MKDEs is also implemented. Output can be exported in VTK and XDMF formats for 3D visualization in ParaView, VisIt, and Mayavi. The package supports both 2D and 3D MKDE calculation and is now maintained by SDSC.

title: California Condors — 3D Space Use
text:

title: Neural Network Movement Models
text:
The Cognavitron — hover for details · click to navigate
This work dates to my PhD dissertation at Colorado State University, completed in 2006. I mention that not as a historical footnote but because it matters: I have been thinking about neural networks, animal behavior, and landscape ecology for twenty years. The tools have changed beyond recognition — from hand-coded networks fit on desktop workstations to transformer architectures trained on GPU clusters — but the questions I was asking then are the same questions driving my current work. Connectivity. Movement. How animals respond to the landscape they live in. How we build models that are honest about that complexity.
I came to deep learning not through a crash course but through a long arc that started here. That context shapes how I build models, what I think they can and can’t do, and what questions I think are worth asking.
The core idea: use a neural network to model how an animal’s next movement step depends on the surrounding landscape. Step length and turning angle are modeled jointly — step length with a gamma distribution, turning angle with a von Mises distribution. When multiple landscape features compete for influence, a finite mixture structure assigns a mixing weight to each, letting the model capture multi-modal movement decisions.

title: Neural Network Movement Models
text:
Network Architecture
The network takes landscape inputs and outputs the parameters of probability distributions for turning angle and step length. Turning angle is modeled with a von Mises distribution — the circular analogue of the Gaussian, appropriate for angles. When an animal responds to multiple landscape features, a finite mixture assigns a weight to each component, capturing the multi-modal structure of real movement decisions.



Simple mixture network for a single landscape feature. Scalar inputs (distances to the feature) drive the mixing proportion node u₁; vector inputs (directional components s and d) feed the hidden layer outputting von Mises parameters.





Full two-feature mixture network. Two hidden layers; two bias nodes; inputs for two landscape features (s and d components for each). The output layer produces one mixing proportion u₁ and four von Mises parameters w₁–w₄. Each row of weights encodes how a specific feature influences turning direction at a given moment.





Example von Mises mixture output — the predicted turning angle distribution for one animal at one step. The bimodal shape reflects a real behavioral decision: the animal is balancing attraction toward one feature against another. A unimodal distribution would miss this entirely.

title: Neural Network Movement Models
text:
Training with a Genetic Algorithm
By 2004–2006, gradient-based optimization of neural networks was well-known but practically difficult — vanishing gradients, sensitivity to initialization, and local minima were real problems, especially with small ecological datasets and non-standard output distributions. I trained the networks using a genetic algorithm (GA): a population of candidate weight vectors evolves over generations through selection, crossover, and mutation, with fitness measured by negative log-likelihood on the training data.



GA operations: elitism preserves the best chromosomes across generations; selection chooses parents by fitness; crossover recombines weight vectors; mutation introduces variation. Each chromosome is a full set of network weights.





GA training history over 50,000 generations. Negative log-likelihood (y-axis) for the minimum (black), mean (gray), and maximum (light gray) chromosomes in the population. The sharp initial drop reflects rapid early improvement; the long tail reflects the difficulty of the optimization landscape. Population diversity (spread between min and max) is maintained throughout.

title: Neural Network Movement Models
text:
Agent-Based Simulation Framework
The fitted network was embedded in an agent-based model (ABM) to simulate animal movement through heterogeneous landscapes. The GA is not just a training tool — it is part of the model architecture, iterating within the ABM to fit parameters to observed movement data for each individual animal.



Full model architecture. The Spatial Component supplies continuous and categorical landscape rasters. The Move Agent Component contains the neural network, the GA optimizer, and the effector that generates movement steps. The Interaction Component mediates between landscape and agent. Solid arrows: always active; dashed: fitting; double-dashed: simulation.

title: Neural Network Movement Models
section: Simulations — Puma Movement in Southern California
text:
Simulations — Puma Movement in Southern California
The fitted model was validated by simulation: run the trained agent forward through the landscape and compare the simulated paths to observed movement data. The puma data used here were generously shared by Paul Beier (Northern Arizona University), whose long-term field work on mountain lion (Puma concolor) movement and landscape connectivity in southern California provided an ideal test case — a large-bodied carnivore navigating a highly fragmented urban-wildland matrix.



Individual simulated paths for puma M8 — five trials, each shown as points and lines over 1993 land cover. The model was fit to M8’s observed GPS locations; these are forward simulations using the fitted parameters. The paths follow habitat structure, avoiding urban core and tracking open space and habitat/vacant land cover.





Same simulations at a broader spatial extent, showing the full southern California urban matrix. The simulated paths stay coherent with the observed movement corridor — navigating through habitat patches in a landscape dominated by urban and agricultural land use.





All simulated paths combined — points and lines for all trials overlaid. The density of simulated locations reveals the movement corridors the model learned from the observed data: concentrated in open space and habitat patches, thinning across agricultural land, absent from urban core.


More content coming soon.

title: Red Diamond Rattlesnakes
text:
The Cognavitron — hover for details · click to navigate
My M.S. research at UC San Diego tracked red diamond rattlesnakes (Crotalus ruber) in the coastal chaparral of San Diego County — radio-telemetry, home range estimation, and an early attempt to model their movement statistically. It was also where I learned that fieldwork has a way of creating situations no one planned for.

title: Red Diamond Rattlesnakes
text:
Study Area
The study sites were fragments of coastal sage scrub and chaparral embedded in the urban matrix of northern San Diego — canyon reserves surrounded on all sides by residential development. The urban-wildland interface was not a backdrop; it was the research question. Were snakes in fragmented patches behaving differently from those in larger core areas? Were their home ranges smaller, their movements more constrained?



Coastal chaparral in northern San Diego County — the dominant vegetation type in the study area. Native shrub cover over rocky terrain; dense, difficult to move through, and home to a surprising diversity of wildlife.





The urban-wildland interface at one of the study sites. Native chaparral in the foreground; residential development immediately behind the ridge. Several of the study animals ranged up to — and occasionally beyond — this boundary.

title: Red Diamond Rattlesnakes
text:
Field Methods
Snakes were located by radio-telemetry and GPS, typically multiple times per week. Each animal carried a small implanted transmitter — surgically placed under the skin along the body wall — that broadcast a unique radio frequency. I’d hike the canyons with a receiver and directional antenna, triangulating on the signal, and close in on foot until I found the snake under a rock, in a crevice, or coiled in the shade of a shrub.



A red diamond rattlesnake (Crotalus ruber) close-up. The heat-sensing pit organs between eye and nostril are clearly visible — the defining feature of pit vipers, used to detect warm-bodied prey in complete darkness.

title: Red Diamond Rattlesnakes
text:
The Coachwhip
One day my labmate John came back from tracking horned lizards with an unusual problem. The lizard he’d been tracking had gone silent — and when he finally found the signal, it wasn’t moving. He dug around and found a coachwhip (Masticophis flagellum, now Coluber flagellum) that had swallowed the lizard, transmitter and all. We brought the snake to the lab and took an X-ray. There it was: the outline of the horned lizard, and the transmitter clearly visible inside.



Red racer (Coluber flagellum piceus, formerly Masticophis flagellum piceus) — the subspecies found in coastal southern California. Fast, alert, and opinionated. (© Patrick Briggs, californiaherps.com)





X-ray of the coachwhip (Masticophis flagellum) after swallowing the radio-tagged horned lizard. The transmitter is clearly visible inside the snake. This image is what started the conversation about what to do next.


Radio transmitters are not cheap, and they can be refurbished. My advisor, Ted Case — a serious herpetologist and ecologist — looked at the situation practically: “Kill the snake, get the transmitter.”
I hated that idea. Coachwhips are beautiful snakes, and this one had already demonstrated her personality — she’d bitten me three times in the nose when we caught her. I asked Ted if I could try to remove the transmitter surgically instead. He thought about it and said okay.
By this point I had implanted transmitters in rattlesnakes on my own. But removing a foreign object — with a horned lizard still partly around it — was different. I anesthetized the snake, made the incision, and worked the transmitter free. I sutured her back up and thought: I must have killed her.
She came around slowly. We kept her in the lab to recover — coachwhips are ectotherms; they don’t need to eat much, and healing moves at the pace of their metabolism. After about two weeks, I caught a fence lizard and offered it to her. She struck, turned it in her coils, and swallowed it cleanly. No hesitation. We gave her a few more weeks, then released her exactly where John had found her.
About nine months later, John was out tracking horned lizards in the same area. He spotted a coachwhip moving through the scrub. Scar on her side. Looked gravid. Moving like she owned the place.

title: Red Diamond Rattlesnakes
text:
Teddy
Before I could track snakes in the field, I needed to learn to implant transmitters surgically. The first one I did was under the supervision of a veterinarian from UCSD.
The snake was a southern Pacific rattlesnake (Crotalus oreganus helleri) — a different species from my study animals, but the procedure is the same. I named him Teddy, after my advisor Ted Case. It seemed appropriate.



Teddy on a snake hook right after capture. A beautiful southern Pacific rattlesnake (Crotalus oreganus helleri) — heavy-bodied, calm, and about to become my first surgical patient. (Photo: Jeff A. Tracey)





The radio transmitter used in the implant surgeries. Quarter shown for scale — small enough to fit inside a snake, expensive enough that losing one was a real problem.





Teddy in the lab prior to surgery. Southern Pacific rattlesnakes (Crotalus oreganus helleri) are a robust, heavy-bodied species — a good candidate for a first implant.


The procedure: anesthetize the snake, make a small incision along the lower body wall, place the transmitter, suture closed. Under a vet’s guidance the first time, then on my own after that. The antenna exits through the skin and trails behind the snake — the signal is what you follow through the chaparral.



Transmitter implantation surgery. The snake is anesthetized on a sterile drape; the incision is open and the transmitter is being seated. Done under veterinary supervision for the first procedure, then independently thereafter.


Teddy recovered well, carried his transmitter, and eventually went back to his home range. My kids got to meet him before he did. I missed him when the study was over.

title: Red Diamond Rattlesnakes
text:
Capturing Crotalus ruber
Before a snake could be fitted with a transmitter, it had to be caught. Red diamond rattlesnakes are generally calm for their size, but handling them requires practice and respect. Snakes were captured by hand using tongs and a snake hook, bagged for transport to the lab, and released at their capture site after recovery from surgery.



Jeff Tracey capturing a red diamond rattlesnake (Crotalus ruber) on San Diego Safari Park property — one of the study sites. (Photo: field crew)

title: Red Diamond Rattlesnakes
text:
Surgery on Crotalus ruber
The images below show transmitter implantation surgery on an actual study animal — a red diamond rattlesnake (Crotalus ruber). These procedures were done with serious preparation: I studied rattlesnake anatomy using specimens from the San Diego Natural History Museum, learned suture technique from a physician (Victor Lipp, MD), worked through veterinary medicine and surgery texts, practiced on road-killed rattlesnakes before operating on live animals, and learned safe handling from the staff at the Reptile House at the San Diego Zoo. The animals were anesthetized, the surgery was performed under sterile conditions, and all individuals were monitored through recovery before being released.



Transmitter implantation surgery on a red diamond rattlesnake (Crotalus ruber). The snake is anesthetized and positioned on a sterile field. Gloved hands steady the animal while the incision is prepared along the lower body wall.





The transmitter seated in the incision prior to suturing closed. The antenna will exit through the skin and trail behind the snake — a flexible wire a few centimeters long, barely visible in the field but trackable by radio.


I never lost a patient. They all recovered quickly.



Radio-tracking in the field. The handheld directional antenna picks up the transmitter signal; the receiver gives signal strength. You follow the beeps through the chaparral until you find the snake.





Collecting GPS locations at a rattlesnake position. Each location fix required standing directly over the snake with a survey-grade GPS antenna — 300 fixes per visit, averaged to get a precise position. The snake is somewhere in the chaparral below. (Photo: field crew)

title: Red Diamond Rattlesnakes
text:
Movement and Home Range
The main thesis work asked whether rattlesnake movement patterns differed between fragmented and core habitat patches. Males and females also differed substantially — males move more and farther, particularly during the breeding season.



Distributions of turning angles (a) and move lengths (b) for female and male Crotalus ruber. Males show much larger maximum move lengths and a broader distribution — consistent with active mate-searching during the breeding season.





Mean move length (m), maximum squared distance from the centroid (m²), and minimum convex polygon area (m²) for Crotalus ruber — three measures of movement and space use from Chapter 2 of my M.S. thesis. (Tracey 2000)





Movement path of rattlesnake M04 overlaid on land cover — natural vegetation in green, developed areas in orange and yellow, urban matrix in gray. The snake’s movements are tightly clustered within a small patch of natural habitat surrounded by development, illustrating the fragmentation question at the core of the study.





Home range (Terra Nova study site) for one individual, estimated by minimum convex polygon (MCP) and kernel density estimation. The land cover map shows the fragmented nature of the habitat — natural vegetation (green) surrounded by urban development.

title: sUAV Vegetation Mapping
text:
The Cognavitron — hover for details · click to navigate




Multispectral and RGB orthophotos and 3D surface models acquired with a DJI Mavic 3M over southern California chaparral. The goal is to map and monitor vegetation cover, detect invasive species, and characterize habitat structure at ecologically relevant scales — work that is difficult or impossible with satellite imagery alone.
The images below are from a mission flown at Rancho Mission Canyon, San Diego County, California on May 6, 2026.



RGB orthophoto of Rancho Mission Canyon chaparral. Trail system visible; dominant shrub cover includes lemonade berry (Rhus integrifolia) and other native chaparral species. Acquired with DJI Mavic 3M, processed with WebODM.





False-color multispectral orthophoto (R-G-NIR) of the same area. Blue tones indicate healthy vegetation with high NIR reflectance; yellow-green and darker tones indicate stressed or senesced tissue. The spectral contrast reveals fine-scale variation in vegetation condition not visible in the RGB image.





3D surface model of Rancho Mission Canyon generated by structure-from-motion photogrammetry (WebODM). Canopy height and terrain structure are clearly resolved across the slope. The trail system and individual shrub crowns are visible.

title: sUAV + Mask R-CNN — Aerial Wildlife Detection
text:
The Cognavitron — hover for details · click to navigate




Mask R-CNN instance segmentation applied to nadir UAV imagery for detection and classification of marine wildlife from aerial surveys. The model detects and delineates individual animals in high-resolution ocean imagery — leopard sharks, bat rays, sea lions, seabirds — at confidence levels suitable for automated survey pipelines.



Leopard shark (Triakis semifasciata) detection. Confidence: 1.00. The instance segmentation mask captures the full body outline against the ocean surface.





Bat ray (Myliobatis californica) detection. Confidence: 0.99. Partial view — the ray is at the edge of the frame — and the model still segments it correctly.





California sea lion (Zalophus californianus) detection. Confidence: 0.98. Swimming at the surface; the segmentation mask distinguishes the animal from sun glint and surface texture.





Seabird in flight detection. Confidence: 0.96. The model handles a flying animal against open water — a harder case than a stationary surface animal. Note the large aggregation of animals visible in the upper portion of the frame that were not detected — training data limitations on dense aggregations.


More content coming soon.

title: Jeff A. Tracey, PhD
text: Research Associate at Conservation Biology Institute

title: Jeff A. Tracey, PhD
text:
The Mission
We are at an unprecedented time. Never in human history has biodiversity — life on planet Earth — been under greater threat.
At the same time, never before have we had more powerful tools for gathering data from the wild and processing it to yield more useful insights and guide our decisions.
We simultaneously face a threat and have an opportunity.
My mission is to bring AI and other computational tools to bear on ecological research and conservation science so that we may effectively meet the challenge of protecting our home and the life we share it with.
Read more about the mission →




California sea lions at a southern California haulout (Photo: Jeff A. Tracey 2026)

title: Jeff A. Tracey, PhD
text:
What I Bring
What I Do: I build machine learning tools for wildlife monitoring — working across acoustic recordings, camera trap imagery, and UAV-based remote sensing, often under the messy, class-imbalanced conditions that real-world ecological datasets produce.
Background: My background spans over 20 years, with 15 years at USGS — a decade as an ecologist and biostatistician at the Western Ecological Research Center, then as a computational scientist with Advanced Research Computing — which means I’ve worked on both sides of the field-to-algorithm pipeline. I’m currently affiliated with the Conservation Biology Institute.
Current focus: deep generative models for acoustic data augmentation, geospatial AI for sUAV (drone) survey data, and multi-modal approaches to species detection and behavioral analysis.
My full story →



Mask R-CNN detections of marine animals on aerial ocean imagery that I captured with a DJI Air 3S. (Imagery and models by Jeff A. Tracey)
